response_text:
The alarm screamed at 4:30 AM, a sound that felt like an indictment. For the past three years, my idea of work had been the fluorescent buzz of a call center, the desperate politeness in a customer’s voice, and the silent, solitary tally of minutes until my next break. Work was a transaction: my time for their money, a daily erosion. Today was different. Today, I was reporting to the gates of the Consolidated Shipping warehouse, a new hire for the local union. I had no expectations, only a vague hope that the pay would be better.

The difference was immediate, visceral. It wasn’t the cavernous space of the warehouse, or the scent of diesel and cardboard, but the sound. Instead of the hushed, anxious murmur of cubicles, there was a loud, echoing camaraderie. Men and women in steel-toed boots and worn jeans called to each other across the concrete floor, their voices bouncing off the high ceilings. They weren’t just colleagues; they were a crew. My new supervisor, a broad-shouldered woman named Rita with a grip that could crack walnuts, handed me my assignment and a bright orange vest. “Stick with Pete,” she said, jerking her thumb toward a man about my dad’s age. “He’ll show you the ropes. And remember, kid, you’re union now. You work safe, you work smart, and you take your breaks. All of ‘em.”
